questions.question: Based on paragraphs 10-12, what can you infer was important to Margaret as a photojournalist?

responses.content: Black-and-white film at that time was sensitive to blue light, not the reds and oranges of hot steel. The pictures came out all black. Margaret solved this problem by bringing along a new style of flare (which produces white light) and having assistants hold them to light her scenes. Her abilities resulted in some of the best steel factory pictures of that era, and these earned her national attention.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is within the character limit and directly answers the question by showing how Margaret's hardworking attitude helped her solve a technical problem and gain national attention. The highlighted text is a valid, complete answer that demonstrates her perseverance and problem-solving skills, which advanced her career.

questions.question: The author includes several examples of Bourke-White being the “first” or “only” person to do something. Why do you think the author chose to include these details?

responses.content: Over time she adapted her techniques to photograph people and was adept at catching expressions and showing hardship. In 1930, she was the first photographer from a Western country to be allowed into the Soviet Union (now Russia), where she took pictures of the workers in what was then a communist country.
